one_line_takeaway: A broad 8-model empirical study showing LLMs emit vulnerable code at ~10-42% rates, that newer/bigger models are not necessarily safer, and that self-generated hints and 'explained' repair feedback help security only when hints are relevant/precise and the model can follow instructions.
key_finding: All eight models frequently generate vulnerable code, with AllV-R ranging roughly 9.8% to 42.1% across models and datasets. Rates are much higher on the diverse SecurityEval (avg TarV-R 20.7%, AllV-R 33.4%) than on SecCodePLT (avg TarV-R 8.5%, AllV-R 13.2%). Counterintuitively, code-optimized/older models (CodeLlama-34B, StarCoder2-15B) are often SAFER than newer general models: GPT-4o has a higher vanilla vulnerability rate than GPT-3.5 (SecCodePLT TarV-R 15.0% vs 7.7%; SecurityEval 25.6% vs 12.4%). Self-generated hints help only when relevant and precise: GPT-4o produces precise hints 98.6% of the time and gains a 12.4-point TarV-R drop, whereas CodeLlama-7B's hints are precise only 46.5% of the time and yield little or negative benefit; irrelevant hints (missing the target CWE) generally INCREASE the vulnerability rate. Contextualized hints outperform bare CWE definitions. For repair, explained feedback beats direct feedback but only for strong instruction-followers (GPT-4o, GPT-3.5, DeepSeek-Coder-V2); weaker models barely improve under either feedback type.
core_contribution: An empirical study (not a new tool or defense) that systematically measures how eight instruction-tuned LLMs behave across three security dimensions of code generation: (1) their inherent tendency to emit vulnerable code under plain 'vanilla' prompts, (2) whether augmenting the prompt with the model's OWN self-generated CWE vulnerability hints prevents insecure code, and (3) how well the models repair their own vulnerable code when given two levels of CodeQL-derived feedback (raw 'direct' feedback vs. GPT-4o-'explained' feedback). Distinctively, the study spans open-weight and smaller-scale models (3B-34B) alongside proprietary GPT-3.5/GPT-4o, filling a gap the authors identify in prior work that focused narrowly on large closed models. Key measured claims: self-generated hints only help when they are both relevant (contain the target CWE) and precise (match the official CWE definition), and explained feedback beats direct feedback for repair on capable, instruction-following models.
threat_model: Not an adversarial attack/defense paper; the concern is unintentional introduction of exploitable weaknesses. The implicit threat model: a developer prompts an LLM for functional code without security requirements, the model silently produces code containing CWE-classified vulnerabilities (e.g., path traversal CWE-22, code injection CWE-94/95, SQL injection CWE-89), and the developer deploys it, creating exploitable attack surface. The paper measures the frequency/type of such flaws and evaluates prompt-time prevention and post-hoc repair as mitigations. There is no active adversary manipulating the model in the experiments.
limitations: Authors' stated threats to validity: (1) detection relies solely on CodeQL static analysis, which misses dynamic/runtime-triggered vulnerabilities and can mislabel; (2) Python-only, so findings may not transfer to C++/Java; (3) only two benchmarks, which may not cover the full vulnerability space; (4) a static, single-turn setup that does not capture the multi-turn iterative debugging real developers do. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: preciseness judging and explained-feedback generation both depend on GPT-4o (potential self-favoring bias since GPT-4o is also an evaluated model); 6 CWEs / 274 SecCodePLT samples were dropped for CodeQL incompatibility; and functional correctness of the 'secure' code is not verified, so vulnerability reduction could trade off against broken functionality.

datasets_used: SecCodePLT (1,071 samples used, 21 CWEs, after excluding 274 samples for 6 CWEs unsupported by CodeQL; original dataset 1,345 questions mutated from 5 seeds per CWE across 27 CWEs)
SecurityEval (121 coding problems, 69 CWEs, real-world + expert-annotated)
CodeQL used as the static vulnerability-detection oracle
HumanEval and MBPP cited for the models' baseline coding accuracy (model-selection context, not the study's own experiments)
quantitative_results: Vanilla-prompt AllV-R spans ~9.8% to 42.1% across models/datasets (abstract range 9.8%-42.1%)
SecCodePLT averages: TarV-R 8.5%, AllV-R 13.2%; SecurityEval averages: TarV-R 20.7%, AllV-R 33.4%
GPT-4o vanilla SecCodePLT TarV-R 15.0% / AllV-R 18.0% (highest on SecCodePLT); DeepSeekV2-16B highest on SecurityEval at TarV-R 27.3% / AllV-R 42.1%
GPT-4o > GPT-3.5 in vulnerability (worse): SecCodePLT TarV-R 15.0% vs 7.7%, SecurityEval TarV-R 25.6% vs 12.4%
Self-generated hints: GPT-4o TarV-R drops 12.4 points on both datasets (SecCodePLT AllV-R -5.5, SecurityEval AllV-R -17.3); CodeLlama-7B and several others show minimal or negative change
Hint preciseness (GPT-4o-as-judge): GPT-4o 98.6%, GPT-3.5-turbo 92.7%, DeepSeek-Coder-V2-16B 82.6%, Llama3.1-8B 79.7%, StarCoder2-15B 75.0%, Llama3.2-3B 72.5%, CodeLlama-34B 67.4%, CodeLlama-7B 46.5%
Contextualized hints (target-only) on SecurityEval: GPT-4o TarV-R -17.3 (to 8.3%) / AllV-R -19.3; Llama3.1-8B AllV-R -19.0; consistently beat bare CWE-definition hints

Post-hoc repair with explained feedback: GPT-4o SecurityEval AllV-R -28.1 (to 13.2%), SecCodePLT AllV-R -14.3 (to 3.7%); DeepSeekV2-16B SecurityEval AllV-R -21.4 (to 20.7%); GPT-3.5 SecurityEval AllV-R -13.2 (to 6.6%); explained > direct on capable models, weak models ~0 change
GPT-4o-as-judge validation: 95.65% precision / 91.67% recall vs 2 human annotators on 80 hint-definition pairs; explained feedback >98% correct on 160 validated samples
baselines_compared: Vanilla (no-security-requirement) prompt = the baseline condition for all comparisons
Eight LLMs compared against each other: CodeLlama-Instruct 7B, CodeLlama-Instruct 34B, Llama3.1-8B, Llama3.2-3B, StarCoder2-15B, DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 16B, GPT-3.5-turbo-0125, GPT-4o-0513
Prevention conditions compared: vanilla vs. self-generated top-5 hints vs. target-only CWE-Definition hints vs. Contextualized target hints
Repair conditions compared: direct CodeQL feedback vs. GPT-4o-explained feedback
